request.question: A mythical forest creature that is seductive and from Sweden has the same being as what divine figure?

title: Shaykh Haydar
Chullachaki
Nariphon
Skogsrå
Silili
Cura (mythology)
Thalna
Almaz (mythical beast)
Oceanus
Hulder
sentences: Shaykh Haydar or Sheikh Haydar (Persian: شیخ حیدر‎ ‎ "Shaikh Ḥaidar"; b. 1459, Amed - d. 9 July 1488, Tabasaran) was the successor of his father (Shaykh Junayd) as leader of the Safaviyya from 1460-1488.
Haydar maintained the policies and political ambitions initiated by his father.

Under Sheikh Haydar, the Safaviyya became crystallized as a political movement with an increasingly extremist heterodox Twelver Shi'i coloring and Haydar was viewed as a divine figure by his followers.

Shaykh Haydar was responsible for instructing his followers to adopt the scarlet headgear of 12 gores commemorating The Twelve Imams, which led to them being designated by the Turkish term Qizilbash "Red Head".

The Chullachaki (Quechua, "one-footed", from "chulla" or "ch'ulla" = single, odd, unpaired, asymmetric, "chaki" = foot; spelling sometimes also used in Spanish) or Chullachaqui (hispanicized spelling), also known as the Shapishico, is a mythical forest creature of the Peruvian and Brazilian Amazonian jungle.
The Nariphon (Thai: นารีผล ), also known as Makkaliphon (Thai: มักกะลีผล , from Pali "makkaliphala"), is a tree in Buddhist mythology which bears fruit in the shape of young female creatures.
The maidens grow attached by their head from the tree branches.
This tree grows at the Himaphan, a mythical forest where the female fruits are enjoyed by the Gandharvas who cut the fruits and take them away.

The skogsrå , (Forest Rå), "Skogsfrun" (The Mistress of the Forest), "Skogssnuvan", "Skogsnymfen" (Forest Nymph), "Råndan" (the Rå) or "huldra", was a mythical creature of the forest, or Rå, in Swedish folklore.
Silili is a divine figure mentioned in Tablet VI of the Epic of Gilgamesh.
Gilgamesh objects to the unwanted attractions of the goddess Ishtar, enumerating the previous lovers of Ishtar, and the misfortunes which befell them.
He claims that Ishtar once loved the horse, but ultimately decreed a number of misfortunes for him, including the continuous wailing of "his mother, Silili".
Because Silili is only mentioned in passing, there is little to say of her character, other than she is a female divine figure closely associated with the horse.
Cura is the name of a divine figure whose name means "Care" or "Concern" in Latin.
Hyginus seems to have created both the personification and story for his "Fabulae", poem 220.
In Etruscan religion and myth, Thalna was a divine figure usually regarded as a goddess of childbirth.

Determinate gender, however, is not necessarily a characteristic of Etruscan deities, and Thalna is also either depicted as male, or seems to be identified as a male figure because of the placement of names around a scene.
Her other functions include friendship and prophecy.
Her name may mean "growth, bloom."
She appears in Etruscan art in the company of Turan, Tinia, and Menrva.

The almaz (Chechen: "almaz" or "hun-sag"; Adyghe: "almesti"), roughly translated as various "feral forest-man" or "stone spirit", is a mythical beast that is considered to be an evil forest creature with magical powers residing in its hair that exists in Chechen and Ingush folk beliefs and Circassian folk beliefs.
The first "attestation" of it in writing was by a Bavarian captive of the Mongols, but it is present in the national folklore of Chechens and Ingush and of Circassians.

The male almaz is said to be hairy and hideous, and have an axe embedded in its chest, while the female is very beautiful with large breasts and golden hair, and has a "favorite pastime" of dancing naked at night under the moon.
The almaz is said to have magical powers residing in its hair, but if the hair is removed or even grabbed, it may be rendered helpless.

It has been theoried by some to have arisen under Mongolian influence, either during the Mongol invasions of Dzurdzuketia or the intervening period where the northern Dzurdzuk state of Simsir was subjugated to the Mongol-controlled Golden Horde.
The word "almaz" is a loan from Mongolian where it originally meant "forest man".
Amjad Jaimoukha however suggested that the name "almaz" may have started to have been used by North Caucasians for an already existent native concept during the sojourn of the Golden Horde of Simsir.

Oceanus ( ; Greek: Ὠκεανός "Ōkeanós", ] ), also known as Ogenus ("Ogenos", Ωγηνος) or Ogen (Ωγην), was a divine figure in classical antiquity, believed by the ancient Greeks and Romans to be the divine personification of the sea, an enormous river encircling the world.
A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.
(Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)
In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).
She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.
Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: A mythical forest creature that is seductive and from Sweden has the same being as what divine figure?
hop1_docs: Hulder | A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.  (Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)  In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).  She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.  Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

Gryttie | Gryttie is a mythical creature which is reputed to dwell in the depths of Gryttjen, in Hälsingland, Sweden.  Gryttjen Lake is located fairly deep in the forest between the towns of Ljusdal and Hudiksvall.

List of Greek mythological figures | The following is a list of gods, goddesses and many other divine and semi-divine figures from Ancient Greek mythology and Ancient Greek religion.  (The list does not include creatures; for these, see List of Greek mythological creatures.)

Peder Jönsson | Peder Jönsson (died 1640), was a Swedish hunter and fisherman from Söderköping, who was executed for having confessed to sexual intercourse with a Sjörået (a lake-nymph; a mythical female creature of the lake).  Though they are other cases of the same kind in 17th-century Sweden, his was the only case were the sentence is confirmed to have been executed.

Iele | The Iele are feminine mythical creatures In Romanian mythology.  There are several differing descriptions of their characteristics.  Often they are described as virgin fairies ("zâne" in Romanian), with great seductive power over men, with magic skills and attributes similar to Nymphs, Naiads and Dryads found in Greek mythology.  They are also similar to the Samodivas in Bulgaria

Coco (folklore) | The Coco (or Cuco, Coca, Cuca, Cucuy) is a mythical ghost-monster, equivalent to the bogeyman, found in many Hispanic and Lusophone countries.  He can also be considered a Hispanic version of a bugbear, as it is a commonly used figure of speech representing an irrational or exaggerated fear.  The Coco is a male being while Coca is the female version of the mythical monster, although it is not possible to distinguish one from the other as both are the representation of the same being.

Pombero | The Pombéro is a mythical humanoid creature of small stature being from Guaraní mythology.  The legend, along with other mythological figures from the Guaraní, is an important part of the culture in the region spanning from northeast Argentina northward through the whole of Paraguay and southern Brazil.  The Pombéro is said to capture particularly ungrateful girls, and force them to kiss him, and later, force them to have sexual intercourse with him.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Hulder — [1]: "A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore."
- skogsrå — [1]: "skogsrå \"forest spirit\""
- Huld — [1]: "völva divine figure Huld"
- Holda — [1]: "German Holda"

Ambiguities:
- Multiple labels/variants for the creature: "hulder", "huldra", "skogsrå", "Tallemaja".
- Two divine names are given as equivalents: "Huld" and "Holda" (both in [1]).

Bridge / missing fact:
- None missing — passage [1] explicitly states the hulder "is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda." Final conclusion: the hulder is the same being as Huld (and the German Holda).

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Hulder"
- Open page "Huld" (search "Huld völva" if needed)
- Open page "Holda"
- (Optional) "Skogsrå" or "Huldra" pages for variant names
hop2_docs: Hulder | A hulder is a seductive forest creature found in Scandinavian folklore.  (Her name derives from a root meaning "covered" or "secret".)  In Norwegian folklore, she is known as huldra ("the [archetypal] hulder", though folklore presupposes that there is an entire Hulder race and not just a single individual).  She is known as the skogsrå "forest spirit" or Tallemaja "pine tree Mary" in Swedish folklore, and ulda in Sámi folklore.  Her name suggests that she is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.

Huld | In Scandinavian mythology, Huld is only referenced by "völva" or "seiðkona", that is a woman who practiced the seiðr.  She is mentioned in the "Ynglinga saga", "Sturlunga saga" and a late medieval Icelandic tale.  In the latter source, she is Odin's mistress and the mother of the demi-goddesses Þorgerðr and Irpa.  As her name suggests, Huld may be in origin the same being as the Huldra and the German Holda.

Coat of arms of Lardal | The coat of arms of Lardal was approved on 17 July 1992 and is a gold hulder on a red field.  The flag was at the same time approved as a yellow hulder on a red field.  Both the coat of arms and flag were designed by the artist Arvid Sveen.

Hafdís Huld | Hafdís Huld Þrastardóttir (born 22 May 1979), known simply as Hafdís Huld, is an Icelandic singer and actress.  She began her musical career as a member of the electronic band GusGus in 1995 and left the group in 1999.  Hafdís made her solo debut with her 2006 album "Dirty Paper Cup".

Völva | A vǫlva or völva (Old Norse and Icelandic, respectively; plural forms "vǫlur" and "völvur"), sometimes anglicized vala; also spákona or spækona) is a female shaman and seer in Norse religion and a recurring motif in Norse mythology.

Palle Huld | Palle Huld (2 August 1912 – 26 November 2010) was a Danish film actor and writer.  He appeared in 40 films between 1933 and 2000.  He was born in Hellerup in Denmark.  His journey around the world at the age of 15 in 1928 reportedly inspired Hergé to create Tintin.

Völuspá | Völuspá (Old Norse Vǫluspá or Vǫluspǫ́, "Prophecy of the Völva (Seeress)"; reconstructed Old Norse ] , Modern Icelandic ] ) is the first and best known poem of the Poetic Edda.  It tells the story of the creation of the world and its coming end, related to the audience by a völva addressing Odin.  It is one of the most important primary sources for the study of Norse mythology.  Henry Adam Bellows proposed a 10th-century dating and authorship by a pagan Icelander with knowledge of Christianity.  He also assumes the early hearers would have been very familiar with the "story" of the poem and not in need of an explanation.
response.summary_2: Hulder is originally the same being as the völva divine figure Huld and the German Holda.
